summary: The five foods that cause seminal emission are completed: garlic, cress, purslane, eggs, arugula. The arugula (gargir) digression: it brightens eyes. Rav Huna says eat it or rub it on the eyes. Then a startling report: Rav and Rav Nachman, when traveling, would publicly announce 'who wants to be my wife for today?' The Gemara explains why this was halakhically permissible: public announcement, proper kiddushin, willing women, and scholars' children are identifiable. After this temporary marriage ends, the woman needs seven clean days before remarrying. The mishna then describes the KG's final handoff to the Beit Avtinas family and the all-night vigil keeping him awake through methods ranging from Torah study to young priests snapping their fingers to startle him.

display.title: Open and formal, not secret: the legal basis for Rav's traveling marriages
display.whats: The five emission-causing foods are completed: garlic, cress, purslane, eggs, arugula. The arugula (gargir) digression notes it brightens eyes. Then a startling report: Rav and Rav Nachman, when traveling, would publicly announce 'who wants to be my wife for today?' and marry temporarily. The Gemara explains this controversial practice and its legal basis.
display.hint: The traveling marriage practice attributed to Rav and Rav Nachman seems shocking but the Gemara explains it was a rabbinic tool to permit relations in transit without violating Torah law - using local women who were legal marriage partners (Jews) and formalizing a proper temporary kiddushin.

learning.learnerQuestion: How could Rav and Rav Nachman publicly announce for a 'wife for the day' while traveling without this being a forbidden, casual sexual arrangement?
learning.coreTension: A report this blunt looks at first glance like scandalous behavior from leading Amoraim, so the sugya has to show what specific halakhic structure separates it from forbidden relations.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara isolates the features that make the practice valid: the announcement was public rather than secret, a proper kiddushin was performed, the woman consented freely, and because scholars are publicly recognized, any resulting children would be identifiable rather than at risk of an undetected forbidden union.
learning.resolution: The practice was halakhically valid because it satisfied formal kiddushin requirements under conditions (publicity, scholarly recognition) that closed off the mamzerut concern; the woman then required seven clean days before remarrying.

takeaway.text: An act that looks scandalous on its surface can be halakhically valid if it satisfies every formal requirement of the relevant legal category; the Gemara evaluates the structure of an act, not how it appears.

learning.ahaMoment: What reads as scandalous on first hearing turns out to be a fully structured legal act: open, consensual, formally married, for a defined period. The visibility is exactly what removes the danger.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners often react to the surface shock of 'wife for the day' and assume the Gemara is describing something improper, instead of tracking the specific legal conditions (public announcement, valid kiddushin, scholarly identifiability) that the sugya says make it permitted.
learning.memoryAnchor: Five foods done: garlic, cress, purslane, eggs, arugula. Rav's traveling marriages: publicly announced, proper kiddushin, women fully willing, children identified.

argumentFlow.label: Five emission-causing foods completed
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The five foods are: garlic, cress (shalhin), purslane (halalglogot), eggs, arugula (gargir). Arugula also brightens the eyes and can be rubbed on them.

argumentFlow.label: Rav and Rav Nachman announce for daily wives
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: When Rav traveled to Darshish (or similar places), he would publicly announce: 'Who wishes to be my wife for the day?' Similarly Rav Nachman when traveling announced for a temporary marriage.

argumentFlow.label: Why this is permitted: open announcement + proper kiddushin
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The practice was permissible because: (1) it was announced publicly, not clandestinely; (2) proper kiddushin (formal betrothal/marriage) was performed; (3) the women consented fully; (4) scholars (rabbanan) are publicly known, so their children would be identified and not suspected of mamzerut.

argumentFlow.label: What about children from such unions?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A concern is raised: children from such temporary marriages might not know their father and could inadvertently marry relatives (forbidden unions). The Gemara responds: scholars are well-known; their children are identified.

argumentFlow.label: Such a woman needs seven clean days before remarrying
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: After such a temporary marriage ends (the scholar leaves), the woman must wait seven clean days (shiva nekiyim) before she can marry another man. This is because desire may have caused her to see blood.

sourceRefs.note: Required wait before the woman can remarry

concepts.def: Formal betrothal/marriage. Even a temporary marriage (for a day) requires proper kiddushin to be halakhically valid. Without it, the relationship would be forbidden.
Seven clean days - a woman who saw blood (even from desire) must count seven clean days before she can be with another man. This applies after the temporary marriage ends.

misconceptions.misconception: Rav and Rav Nachman's 'wife for the day' was an informal or casual arrangement, closer to concubinage than marriage.
misconceptions.correction: It was formal kiddushin, requiring the same legal betrothal as any marriage. The arrangement was temporary in duration, not in legal validity.

visualizableElements.desc: Rav arrives in town - public announcement - woman accepts - proper kiddushin performed - they live together for the visit - Rav departs - woman counts seven clean days - she can remarry

quizSeeds.question: What three factors made Rav and Rav Nachman's traveling marriages halakhically valid rather than forbidden relations?
quizSeeds.answer: The announcement was made publicly rather than secretly; proper kiddushin was performed; and because scholars are publicly recognized, their children would be identifiable, removing the mamzerut concern.

quizSeeds.question: Why does the woman need to wait seven clean days after the traveling scholar departs, even though no menstrual period necessarily occurred?
quizSeeds.answer: Desire itself can cause blood to appear (dam teshuka). Since this possibility cannot be ruled out, she counts seven clean days as a precaution before she may marry another man.

sugyot.finalRuling: Five emission-causing foods: complete. Traveling marriage practice: described as halakhically valid under specific conditions (public announcement, proper kiddushin, consenting willing women, scholar's public recognition).

alternateAngles.desc: Some later authorities are uncomfortable with the traveling-marriage reports and read them as non-literal. The Gemara presents them straightforwardly as historical practice.

glossary.en: Formal betrothal/marriage. Even temporary marriages require proper kiddushin.
Seven clean days - counted after any blood-sighting before the woman may be with another man.

display.title: From Torah study to finger-snapping: the escalating all-night vigil
display.whats: The KG is formally handed to the Beit Avtinas family (the hereditary keepers of the incense formula) to be kept awake on the eve of Yom Kippur through the night. Various methods are used to prevent him from sleeping: dancing, music, and keeping him engaged. A Kohen Gadol who is a Torah scholar delivers a dvar Torah; if not a scholar, others learn before him. If he showed signs of wanting to sleep, young priests snap their fingers on the floor to wake him.
display.hint: Sleep on the eve of Yom Kippur creates a halakhic risk: if the KG has a nocturnal emission (tum'at keri) he cannot perform the service. Keeping him awake is therefore a practical halakhic necessity, not just a custom.

learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the mishna devote so much detail to keeping the KG awake all night before Yom Kippur, escalating from Torah study to young priests snapping their fingers at him?
learning.coreTension: If the KG falls asleep and has a nocturnal emission, he becomes ritually impure and cannot perform the next day's service, so the elders need an escalating, reliable system rather than a single fragile safeguard.
learning.coreMove: The mishna lays out a graded response: first engage the KG in something absorbing on its own terms (a Torah lesson, delivered by him if he is a scholar or to him if he is not), and only escalate to physically jarring measures like finger-snapping once drowsiness actually appears.
learning.resolution: The vigil runs in stages, from constructive engagement (Torah study) to direct physical intervention (finger-snapping), continuing until the time arrives to begin the morning service.

takeaway.text: A procedure built to prevent a single failure point often layers escalating safeguards rather than relying on one method, moving from the least intrusive intervention to the most direct only as needed.

learning.ahaMoment: The all-night vigil is functional halakha disguised as ceremony: sleep risks emission, emission disqualifies the KG, so every measure to keep him awake is a precautionary safeguard, not pageantry.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners tend to read the finger-snapping detail as colorful folklore and miss that it sits at the end of a deliberate escalation ladder, used only once gentler engagement (Torah study) has failed to keep the KG alert.
learning.memoryAnchor: Beit Avtinas: KG sworn in, then kept awake. Method escalates: Torah first, then finger-snapping. All to prevent tum'at keri.

argumentFlow.label: KG handed to Beit Avtinas for the eve of Yom Kippur
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: The elders hand the KG to the priestly elders, who escort him to the Beit Avtinas chamber. They adjure him (make him swear) that he will not deviate from their instructions regarding the incense service. Then they leave him.

sourceRefs.note: Handoff to Beit Avtinas and oath

argumentFlow.label: KG is kept awake all night
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: They keep the KG awake throughout the night. If he is a Torah scholar, he gives a dvar Torah. If he is not a scholar but is able to learn, others learn before him. If he begins to doze, young priests snap their fingers on the floor to startle him awake.

concepts.def: The hereditary priestly family responsible for preparing the ketoret (incense) formula. Their chamber in the Temple was where the KG was kept on the eve of Yom Kippur.

requiresUnderstanding: Tum'at keri and its consequences for the Yom Kippur service

misconceptions.misconception: Keeping the KG awake all night before Yom Kippur is a ceremonial tradition of honor.
misconceptions.correction: It is a practical halakhic precaution: sleep risks a nocturnal seminal emission, which would render the KG ritually impure and unable to perform the next day's service.

visualizableElements.desc: Eve of Yom Kippur at Beit Avtinas: oath administered, elders depart, KG alone with young priests. If scholar: gives shiur all night. If not: hears Torah. If sleepy: priests snap fingers on floor to wake him.

quizSeeds.question: Why was the High Priest kept awake all night on the eve of Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.answer: To prevent a seminal emission from sleep (tum'at keri), which would make him ritually impure and unable to perform the Yom Kippur service the next day.

quizSeeds.question: In what order were the methods for keeping the High Priest awake applied, from least to most direct?
quizSeeds.answer: First, constructive engagement: he delivered a Torah lesson if he was a scholar, or others taught Torah before him if he was not. Only once he showed actual signs of dozing did young priests snap their fingers on the floor to startle him.

sugyot.finalRuling: The all-night vigil is mandatory: the KG must stay awake until dawn to avoid tum'at keri. The Beit Avtinas chamber is where this takes place.

glossary.en: The hereditary priestly family responsible for the incense formula, and also the location/chamber where the KG stayed on the eve of Yom Kippur.
Formal betrothal/marriage. Even temporary marriages require proper kiddushin.
Seven clean days - counted after any blood-sighting before the woman may be with another man.
The hereditary priestly family responsible for the incense formula, and also the location/chamber where the KG stayed on the eve of Yom Kippur.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: 'shalhin' - cress (garden cress); one of the five emission-causing foods.
Rashi: 'halaglogot' - purslane; grows at field boundaries without competing crops nearby.
Rashi: 'gargir' - arugula; one of the emission foods, but also used for eye-brightening.
Rashi: 'do not eat eggs' - in excess; eggs cause emission and the KG should avoid them.
Rashi: 'do not sleep in the host's cloak' - if sleeping in the cloak of the host (who had seminal emission), one might find the discharge on the garment and become tamei.
Rashi: 'ki mikale l'Darshish' - when Rav would travel to a place called Darshish (or similar), he would announce for a temporary wife.
Rashi: 'man havya li l'yoma' - 'who will be my wife for the day?' - the announcement Rav would make on arrival in a town.
Rashi: 'this one near that one' - the concern about children from different towns who might marry each other (as siblings).
Rashi: 'rabbanan kala alaihu' - scholars are widely known by reputation, so their children can be identified.
Rashi: 'tzerichah lashevet shiva nekiyim' - she requires seven clean days (after blood from desire) before she can remarry.
Rashi: 'yihud bilvad' - they were alone together but did not have relations; the Gemara clarifies the degree of contact involved.
Rashi: 'since she accepted him' - her desire for him could cause blood (dam teshuka), requiring the seven clean days.
Rashi: 'not the Jewish nation's agents' - segue to the agency question about whether kohanim act as God's agents or Israel's agents in offering sacrifices.
Rashi: 'not to turn things around' - the concern about using the agency question to permit a vow-bound kohen from acting for the vow-holder.
Rashi: 'maseruhu' - they handed him (the KG) to the elders of the priesthood on the eve of Yom Kippur.
Rashi: 'zlekni kehunah' - the elders of the priesthood instructed him in the Temple service specifics.
Rashi: 'he would depart and weep' - explained in the Gemara below (19b): he wept at the implication that they suspected him.
Rashi: 'and they departed weeping' - the elders also wept because they had to suspect him (treating him as a possible Sadducee).
Rashi: 'if he was a Torah scholar, he gave a dvar Torah' - the KG teaches Torah to himself and those present to stay awake all night.
Rashi: 'if not' - if he was not a scholar who could teach, the elders would teach Torah before him.
Rashi: 'they would read before him in the order of the day' - the full Yom Kippur Torah portions.
Rashi: 'in Job, Ezra, Chronicles' - books that engage the heart and prevent sleep.
Rashi: 'beit Avtinas' - the family whose chamber in the Temple was used for this vigil; they also prepared the incense.
Rashi: 'to teach him the hanifa (incense-offering)' - the specific incense-scooping technique for Yom Kippur, which was especially difficult.
Rashi: 'and they adjured him' - they made the KG swear he would not deviate from their instruction (i.e., not perform the Sadducee version of the incense service).
Rashi: 'Ishi' (my Lord) - the respectful address used for the KG by the elders.
Rashi: 'he departs and weeps' - the KG wept because being sworn implies suspicion of Sadducee tendencies.
Rashi: 'if he was a Torah scholar, he gave a lesson' - the vigil methods: scholar teaches, non-scholar hears Torah, sleepy KG is startled by finger-snapping.
Rashi: 'if not a scholar who knows the content but can hear and understand' - if he can follow along with Torah study, others read to him.
Rashi: 'from Job and Ezra and Chronicles' - specifically books that draw the heart. Sleep doesn't come when hearing these.
Rashi: 'Tzarda finger' - the finger next to the thumb (index or middle finger). Pressing it hard against the thumb and releasing causes a loud snap.
Rashi: 'stand up and cool yourself once on the floor' - the young priests say to the KG: stand up and walk to dispel the sleepiness.
Rashi: they would engage him to keep him from sleep by any means - dancing, walking, until the time for the morning tamid slaughter when it was time to begin the Yom Kippur service.
Rashi: summary of the all-night vigil - 'and the young priests kept him occupied with their antics until dawn.'
